title: Quantum optical synthesis of high-dimensional ultrafast frequency-bin qudits
authors: Prasad Koviri, Tomoya Okita, Rina Yabumoto, Yuta Fujihashi, Masahiro Yabuno, Hirotaka Terai, Shigehito Miki, Kali P. Nayak, Ryosuke Shimizu
abstract: Frequency modes of light are one of the most promising platforms that provide access to high-dimensional quantum states amongst different photonic degrees of freedom capable of high-dimensionality, enabling robust, error-tolerant, and scalable quantum optical information systems. We demonstrate engineering of precisely controlled two-photon high-dimensional states entangled in frequency through time-domain Fourier optical synthesis. We generate and convert a continuous broadband frequency-entangled state into a large range of discrete frequency bins suitable for ITU standards, with spacings ranging from 12.5 GHz to 750 GHz, and observe spectral anticorrelations over 38 frequency bins, including intra-bin pure states at a 100 GHz bin spacing. We characterize the full quantum state dimensionality via Schmidt decomposition and observe lower bounds on the frequency-binned Hilbert-space dimensionalities of at least 289, formed by two entangled qudits with dimension 17. Furthermore, we demonstrate quantum nonlocality via frequency correlations in a transmission experiment over a campus-scale two-node fiber network. This work represents a crucial step towards building a versatile and relatively simple way of generating precisely controlled high-dimensional spectral qudits, with the potential of harnessing in wavelength-multiplexed quantum networks, high-dimensional information processing, and communication of quantum states specifically, and fiber-optic quantum remote sensing.
summary: The researchers developed a method to synthesize high-dimensional frequency-bin qudits by manipulating the temporal structure of photon pairs in a bidirectional SPDC setup. They achieved precise control over frequency bin spacing and phase, demonstrating that these high-dimensional states can be reliably transmitted over a 1.3 km fiber network. This approach offers a versatile platform for scalable, wavelength-multiplexed quantum communication and sensing.
main_problem: The challenge of generating precisely controlled, high-dimensional frequency-bin qudits without the high optical losses or limited tunability associated with existing filtering or fixed-architecture platforms.
main_result: Demonstrated the engineering of high-dimensional frequency-entangled states with tunable bin spacing (12.5 GHz to 750 GHz) and phase, achieving a Hilbert-space dimensionality of at least 289 and proving robust distribution over a 1.3 km fiber network.
method: Time-domain Fourier optical synthesis is used to manipulate the temporal structure of a biphoton wave packet via a nonlinear interferometer, effectively controlling the spectral modulation through Fourier duality.
model_or_system: A bidirectional Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) source using a type-II PPMgSLT crystal, where a pump pulse passes through the crystal twice to create temporally separated wave packets.
key_observables: Joint Spectral Intensity (JSI), Schmidt decomposition (Schmidt number K), Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference visibility, and frequency-bin spacing.
important_parameters: Frequency bin spacing (12.5 GHz to 750 GHz), Hilbert space dimension (at least 289), fiber network length (1.3 km), and White Rabbit synchronization precision (28 ps).
main_assumptions: Low-gain regime of SPDC, classical treatment of the pump field, and perfect spatial mode matching between the two crystal passes.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the generation and detection setup including the SPDC source and SNSPDs; Fig 3 illustrates spectral phase tuning via JSI and HOM interference patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of high-dimensional state generation, describes the experimental setup and the Fourier synthesis mechanism, presents the results of spectral engineering and dimensionality characterization, and concludes with a demonstration of state distribution over a campus-scale fiber network.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable, low-loss method for generating and controlling high-dimensional qudits using the frequency domain, which is compatible with existing WDM telecommunications infrastructure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
